questions.question: Which of the following best describes the legal test for determining whether a valid offer has been made?

options.A: The subjective intention of the offeror at the time of making the statement
options.B: The objective test — how a reasonable person in the position of the offeree would understand the statement
options.C: Whether both parties subjectively intended to be bound by the statement
options.D: The oral or written form in which the statement was communicated

questions.explanation: The objective test applies to offer formation: Smith v Hughes [1871] established that the courts look to the outward conduct and words of the parties, not their subjective intentions. A reasonable person standard governs whether a statement amounts to an offer.

questions.question: In Carlill v Carbolic Smoke Ball Co [1893], the Court of Appeal held that the advertisement was a valid offer because:

options.A: It was communicated in writing to identified individuals
options.B: It contained sufficiently certain terms and was intended to be legally binding, as evidenced by the deposit of £1,000
options.C: The company had previously supplied smoke balls to members of the public
options.D: The offeree had expressly notified the company of her intention to accept

questions.explanation: In Carlill, the advertisement was treated as an offer to the world at large (a unilateral offer) because its terms were sufficiently certain, a £1,000 deposit demonstrated serious intent, and performance of the condition (using the smoke ball) constituted acceptance. The court rejected the argument that it was mere puff.

questions.question: What is an 'invitation to treat' in contract law?

options.A: A binding offer which, once accepted, creates a contract
options.B: A preliminary statement inviting others to make offers, which does not itself constitute an offer
options.C: An offer made under duress and therefore voidable
options.D: A counter-offer that terminates the original offer

questions.explanation: An invitation to treat is a pre-contractual statement that invites others to make offers. It does not constitute an offer and cannot be accepted so as to form a contract. Examples include advertisements (Partridge v Crittenden [1968]), goods displayed in shops (Pharmaceutical Society of GB v Boots [1953]), and catalogues.

questions.question: In Pharmaceutical Society of Great Britain v Boots Cash Chemists [1953], the Court of Appeal held that the display of goods on shelves in a self-service pharmacy was:

options.A: An offer which customers accepted by placing goods in their basket
options.B: An invitation to treat, with the offer being made by the customer at the cash register
options.C: A binding contract formed at the moment of selection
options.D: An offer only in respect of prescription medicines

questions.explanation: The Court of Appeal in Boots held that displaying goods on shelves constitutes an invitation to treat, not an offer. The customer makes the offer when presenting goods at the till, and the contract is formed when the cashier accepts. This ensures the retailer can refuse to sell where necessary (e.g., for legal compliance).

questions.question: Which of the following statements about advertisements is generally correct under Irish and English contract law?

options.A: All advertisements constitute binding offers capable of acceptance
options.B: Advertisements are always invitations to treat and can never be offers
options.C: Advertisements are generally invitations to treat, but can be offers if sufficiently certain and intended to be binding
options.D: Advertisements become offers only once the offeror has received at least one response

questions.explanation: The general rule is that advertisements are invitations to treat (Partridge v Crittenden [1968]). However, an advertisement may constitute an offer where its terms are sufficiently certain and the advertiser intends to be bound — as in Carlill v Carbolic Smoke Ball Co [1893], where the court found a valid unilateral offer to the world.

questions.question: In Harvey v Facey [1893] (PC), the Privy Council held that the telegram 'Lowest price for Bumper Hall Pen £900' was:

options.A: A valid offer which was accepted by Harvey's reply
options.B: An invitation to treat which led to a counter-offer
options.C: Merely a statement of the minimum price, not an offer capable of acceptance
options.D: A conditional offer contingent on Facey receiving a higher bid

questions.explanation: The Privy Council in Harvey v Facey held that 'Lowest price £900' was simply a response to a request for information about price — it was not an offer to sell at that price. Harvey's purported acceptance of that 'price' did not form a contract because Facey had not made any offer to sell.

questions.question: Under the Sale of Goods Act 1893 (Ireland), s58, an auctioneer's call for bids is:

options.A: An offer by the auctioneer which is accepted when a bid is made
options.B: An invitation to treat; each bid is an offer which the auctioneer accepts by the fall of the hammer
options.C: A binding obligation to sell to the highest bidder
options.D: An offer that cannot be revoked once the auction commences

questions.explanation: Under s58 of the Sale of Goods Act 1893, an auction sale is complete on the fall of the auctioneer's hammer. The auctioneer's call for bids is an invitation to treat; each bid constitutes an offer; and the contract is formed when the hammer falls. A bidder may retract a bid before that moment.

questions.question: In Gibson v Manchester City Council [1979] (HL), the House of Lords held that the letter stating the Council 'may be prepared to sell' the house to the plaintiff was:

options.A: A binding offer accepted by Gibson's reply
options.B: An invitation to treat inviting Gibson to make a formal application
options.C: A conditional offer subject to the Council's approval
options.D: An offer which lapsed due to Gibson's failure to respond promptly

questions.explanation: In Gibson v Manchester City Council, the House of Lords rejected the Court of Appeal's finding that a contract existed. The letter using 'may be prepared to sell' was an invitation to treat, not an offer. The council had not committed itself to sell; it was merely inviting Gibson to apply, and no binding offer or acceptance had occurred.

questions.question: What is the general rule regarding the communication of revocation of an offer?

options.A: Revocation is effective from the moment the offeror decides to revoke, regardless of communication
options.B: Revocation must be communicated to the offeree before acceptance, but need not come from the offeror personally
options.C: Revocation is only valid if communicated in writing
options.D: An offer cannot be revoked once communicated to the offeree

questions.explanation: Revocation is effective only when communicated to the offeree before acceptance (Payne v Cave [1789]). Crucially, communication need not come from the offeror personally — in Dickinson v Dodds [1876], reliable third-party communication of revocation was held sufficient, as the offeree had actual knowledge the offer was no longer available.

questions.question: In Dickinson v Dodds [1876], Dodds agreed to keep an offer to sell property open until Friday morning. Before that time, Dickinson heard from a third party that Dodds had sold to another. The Court of Appeal held:

options.A: Dodds was bound to keep the offer open until Friday morning as promised
options.B: Dickinson's attempted acceptance on Thursday was valid because the period had not expired
options.C: The offer was effectively revoked because Dickinson had reliable knowledge from a third party that Dodds had sold to another
options.D: Dodds' promise to keep the offer open created a binding option contract

questions.explanation: In Dickinson v Dodds [1876], the Court of Appeal held that the promise to keep the offer open until Friday was unsupported by consideration and therefore not binding. Once Dickinson had reliable knowledge that the property had been sold to another, the offer was effectively revoked — even though that information came via a third party rather than Dodds himself.

questions.question: The rule in Daulia Ltd v Four Millbank Nominees [1978] provides that in a unilateral contract:

options.A: The offeror may revoke at any time before performance is complete
options.B: Once the offeree has commenced performance, the offeror cannot revoke the offer
options.C: The contract only comes into existence upon full completion of the requested act
options.D: The offeree must notify the offeror before beginning performance

questions.explanation: In Daulia Ltd v Four Millbank Nominees [1978], Goff LJ (obiter) stated that once an offeree has embarked on performance of the requested act in a unilateral contract, the offeror is precluded from revoking the offer. This protects the offeree who has started performing but not yet completed the act.

questions.question: An offer lapses where:

options.A: The offeree fails to accept within the time stipulated, or within a reasonable time where none is stipulated
options.B: The offeree asks a question about the terms of the offer
options.C: The offeror sends a reminder letter
options.D: The offeree begins negotiating with third parties

questions.explanation: An offer lapses if not accepted within the time period specified by the offeror, or, where no period is specified, within a reasonable time. In Ramsgate Victoria Hotel Co v Montefiore [1866], an offer to buy shares lapsed after several months without acceptance because a reasonable time had elapsed. What constitutes a reasonable time depends on the subject matter and circumstances.

questions.question: What is the effect of a counter-offer on the original offer?

options.A: It suspends the original offer pending the offeror's response to the counter-offer
options.B: It varies the terms of the original offer but leaves it intact
options.C: It terminates the original offer, which cannot thereafter be accepted
options.D: It constitutes acceptance subject to a condition

questions.explanation: A counter-offer terminates the original offer. In Hyde v Wrench [1840], Hyde offered to buy Wrench's farm for £950 in response to Wrench's asking price of £1,000. Wrench refused. Hyde then attempted to accept the original £1,000 offer. The court held no contract existed — Hyde's counter-offer had destroyed the original offer, which could not be revived by subsequent purported acceptance.

questions.question: Which of the following distinguishes a mere inquiry or request for information from a counter-offer?

options.A: A mere inquiry asks about alternative terms but does not reject or alter the original offer
options.B: A mere inquiry automatically extends the time for acceptance of the original offer
options.C: A request for information operates as a conditional acceptance
options.D: A mere inquiry must be made in writing to have legal effect

questions.explanation: A mere inquiry or request for information does not destroy the original offer. In Stevenson v McLean [1880], asking whether goods could be delivered over a longer period was treated as a request for information, not a counter-offer, leaving the original offer intact. A counter-offer, by contrast, proposes new or varied terms and rejects the original offer.

questions.question: In Carroll v An Post National Lottery Co [1996] (HC Ireland), Laffoy J held that:

options.A: A scratch card constituted an offer by An Post which was accepted when the card revealed a winning result
options.B: The sale of a scratch card was an offer by the purchaser to play, which An Post accepted by issuing the card
options.C: The rules printed on scratch cards were unfair terms and unenforceable against consumers
options.D: An Post's promotional material was an invitation to treat and no contract was formed until a prize claim was verified

questions.explanation: In Carroll v An Post National Lottery Co [1996], Laffoy J held that the scratch card itself constituted an offer by An Post to pay the prize shown if the card revealed a winning combination. Scratching the card and revealing the winning amount constituted acceptance by performance — a unilateral contract analysis.

questions.question: Under s21 of the Electronic Commerce Act 2000 (Ireland), electronic offers and acceptances are:

options.A: Invalid unless made using a certified electronic signature
options.B: Governed by the same rules as paper offers, with acceptance occurring when the acceptance message is received
options.C: Irrevocable once transmitted electronically
options.D: Only valid between commercial parties, not between a business and a consumer

questions.explanation: The Electronic Commerce Act 2000 (Ireland) confirms that electronic contracts are valid and that general offer and acceptance rules apply. Under s21, an electronic offer and acceptance follow the ordinary rules; acceptance is generally effective when received by the offeror's information system, mirroring instantaneous communication principles from Entores v Miles Far East Corp [1955].

questions.question: The 'battle of the forms' problem arises when:

options.A: Both parties sign and exchange the same standard form contract
options.B: Two parties exchange conflicting standard form contracts, each purporting to be on their own terms
options.C: A party insists on contracting only in writing
options.D: An offer is made orally and acceptance is communicated in writing

questions.explanation: The 'battle of the forms' occurs where each party sends its own standard terms and neither expressly accepts the other's. In Butler Machine Tool Co v Ex-Cell-O Corp [1979], the Court of Appeal applied the traditional offer and counter-offer analysis: the last set of terms sent and acted upon without objection generally governs (the 'last shot' doctrine), though courts may also look at conduct.

questions.question: Which of the following correctly describes the position regarding tenders under Irish law?

options.A: An invitation to tender is always a binding offer to accept the lowest compliant bid
options.B: A tender invitation is generally an invitation to treat, but may carry a collateral obligation to consider conforming tenders
options.C: All tenders submitted in response to an invitation form binding contracts upon submission
options.D: An invitation to tender is an offer only if the tendering party is a government body

questions.explanation: Following Blackpool and Fylde Aero Club v Blackpool BC [1990] and Irish authority, the courts recognise that while an invitation to tender is an invitation to treat (not an offer), it may carry a collateral obligation to consider all conforming tenders. Breach of this obligation can give rise to damages, even though no main contract was formed.

questions.question: What happens to an offer upon the death of the offeror before acceptance?

options.A: The offer is automatically accepted on behalf of the offeror's estate
options.B: The offer generally lapses, particularly where personal performance by the offeror is contemplated
options.C: The offer remains open and may be accepted by the offeree within a reasonable time
options.D: Death of the offeror has no effect on the offer if the offeree is unaware of the death

questions.explanation: The general rule is that an offer lapses on the death of the offeror, especially where personal performance is contemplated (Re Irvine [1928]). An acceptance made after the offeror's death and with knowledge of it will not be effective. Where the offeree is unaware of the death, the position is less certain.

questions.question: In Partridge v Crittenden [1968], an advertisement offering 'bramblefinch cocks and hens 25s each' was held to be:

options.A: A binding offer, so placing an order constituted acceptance and formed a contract
options.B: An invitation to treat, meaning no criminal liability under the Protection of Birds Act 1954 for 'offering for sale'
options.C: A voidable contract because the sale was of protected birds
options.D: An unenforceable contract due to illegality of the subject matter

questions.explanation: In Partridge v Crittenden [1968], the Divisional Court held that the advertisement was an invitation to treat, not an offer for sale. As the defendant had not made an 'offer' to sell within the meaning of the Protection of Birds Act 1954, he was not guilty of the offence of unlawfully offering protected birds for sale.

questions.question: In Billings v Arnott (1945), an Irish case concerning war-time employment, the court applied offer and acceptance principles to hold:

options.A: That an employer's circular about pay increases was a binding offer accepted by employees continuing to work
options.B: That continued performance of existing duties cannot constitute acceptance of a new offer
options.C: That statutory minimum wages displaced any contractual offer
options.D: That acceptance by conduct requires a formal written confirmation

questions.explanation: In Billings v Arnott (1945), the Irish court held that a circular sent by the employer offering enhanced terms was accepted by employees who continued in employment. Continued performance was treated as acceptance by conduct of the employer's offer. This illustrates the Irish courts' willingness to find acceptance through conduct in appropriate circumstances.

questions.question: Which of the following statements about product listings on websites under Irish law is most accurate?

options.A: A product listing on a website is always an offer which is accepted when the customer clicks 'buy'
options.B: A product listing on a website is generally an invitation to treat; the customer's order is the offer, which the seller accepts by confirmation
options.C: Online contracts are invalid without a wet-ink signature under the Electronic Commerce Act 2000
options.D: Acceptance of an online offer is effective only when the seller dispatches the goods

questions.explanation: Consistent with general offer/acceptance principles and the Electronic Commerce Act 2000, a product listing on a website is typically an invitation to treat. The customer's order constitutes the offer; the retailer accepts (forming the contract) typically by sending an order confirmation — not merely an automated acknowledgment of receipt.
